content:
# Genesis Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3r–3v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_004_page_001.jpg, GEN_004_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** The man was intimate with his wife Eve, and she conceived and gave birth to Cain. She said: "I have acquired a man — with the LORD."

**2** She gave birth again — his brother Abel. Abel became a shepherd of flocks; Cain became a worker of the ground.

**3** In the course of time, Cain brought an offering to the LORD from the fruit of the ground.

**4** Abel also brought one — from the firstborn of his flock, from their fat portions. The LORD looked with favor on Abel and his offering,

**5** but on Cain and his offering he did not look with favor. Cain burned with fury, and his face fell.

**6** The LORD said to Cain: "Why are you furious? Why has your face fallen?

**7** If you do well — lift it up. But if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you — but you must master it."

**8** Cain said to his brother Abel, "Let us go out to the field." And when they were in the field, Cain rose up against his brother Abel and killed him.

**9** Then the LORD said to Cain: "Where is your brother Abel?"

He said, "I don't know. Am I my brother's keeper?"

**10** God said: "What have you done? Your brother's blood — it is crying out to me from the ground!

**11** And now: cursed are you from the ground, which opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand.

**12** When you work the ground, it will no longer yield its strength to you. A wanderer and a fugitive you will be on the earth."

**13** Cain said to the LORD: "My punishment is too great to carry."

**14** "Look — today you are driving me off the face of the ground, and from your face I will be hidden. I will be a wanderer and a fugitive on the earth, and whoever finds me will kill me."

**15** The LORD said to him: "Therefore — whoever kills Cain, sevenfold vengeance will be taken on him." And the LORD put a mark on Cain, so that anyone who came across him would not strike him down.

**16** Cain went out from the LORD's presence and settled in the land of Nod — east of Eden.

**17** Cain was intimate with his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to Enoch. He was building a city, and he named the city after his son — Enoch.

**18** To Enoch was born Irad; Irad fathered Mehujael; Mehujael fathered Methushael; Methushael fathered Lamech.

**19** Lamech took two wives: the name of the first was Adah, and the name of the second was Zillah.

**20** Adah gave birth to Jabal — he was the father of those who live in tents and keep livestock.

**21** His brother's name was Jubal — he was the father of all those who play the lyre and the pipe.

**22** Zillah also gave birth — to Tubal-cain, the forger of every cutting tool in bronze and iron. Tubal-cain's sister was Naamah.

**23** Lamech said to his wives:

> Adah and Zillah — hear my voice!
> Wives of Lamech — listen to what I say!
> I have killed a man for wounding me,
> a young man for striking me.
>
> **24** If Cain is avenged sevenfold,
> then Lamech — seventy-seven!

**25** Adam was intimate with his wife again, and she gave birth to a son and named him Seth — saying: "God has granted me another offspring in place of Abel, because Cain killed him."

**26** A son was also born to Seth, and he named him Enosh. At that time, people began to call on the name of the LORD.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On Genesis 4 as a whole — the chapter's shape

Genesis 4 is constructed around three acts of human speech that define the moral world east of Eden. Eve's cry at the first birth (verse 1). Cain's deflection at the first interrogation (verse 9). Lamech's song of preemptive vengeance at the end (verses 23–24). These three utterances form the chapter's spine. Everything between them — the offerings, the murder, the curse, the genealogy — is connective tissue. The chapter begins with new life and ends with an escalating boast of bloodshed. The movement is downward and fast.

The Hebrew prose of this chapter is lean to the point of violence. The narrator does not explain why God rejected Cain's offering. The narrator does not describe the murder. The narrator does not say how Cain felt when he left the LORD's presence. The gaps are not failures; they are the weapon. My translation honors every gap.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3r–3v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana; Dead Sea Scrolls, 4QGen-b and 4QGen-d.*

---

### Verse 1 — "I have acquired a man — with the LORD"

This is the most theologically loaded sentence in the chapter and one of the most disputed in the Torah. The Hebrew is: קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת־יְהוָה (qaniti ish et-YHWH). Every word is contested.

**קָנִיתִי (qaniti)** — the root קָנָה (qanah) means to acquire, to create, to possess, to get. It is the same root used of God as "Creator/Possessor of heaven and earth" (Genesis 14:19) and in Proverbs 8:22, "The LORD acquired me [Wisdom] at the beginning of his work." Eve uses a powerful, rich verb: she has *acquired*, *created*, *brought into being* a man. The word has economic, generative, and theological weight simultaneously. I render "acquired" to preserve the force of the transaction — something has been obtained, something has come into her possession — while carrying the ambiguity of whether it refers to ownership or creation.

**אֶת־יְהוָה (et-YHWH)** — here is where translators have been fighting for two millennia. The preposition אֶת (et) can mean "with," "from," "alongside," "together with," "by means of." Three readings have serious scholarly support:

1. **"with the help of the LORD"** — the most common rendering; Eve acknowledges divine assistance in procreation.
2. **"from the LORD"** — the child was given to her from/by God; this child is divine gift.
3. **"I have acquired a man — the LORD"** — the most radical reading, where אֶת can be read as a direct object marker rather than a preposition. This reading, advocated by some scholars, makes Eve's cry an identification of the newborn with the LORD himself — an astonishing messianic claim at the very first birth in human history. Aquila's Greek translation renders this as ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον τὸν κύριον — "I have acquired a man, the LORD." This cannot be coincidence.

I render "with the LORD" and mark the break with an em dash to make the declaration ring out. Eve does not say this quietly. She shouts it. Something momentous has happened: a human being has made another human being, and she knows the LORD was present in it. The em dash separates the acquisition ("I have acquired a man") from the theological acknowledgment ("with the LORD"), keeping both in tension without flattening either.

The LXX Vaticanus reads: **ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ** — "I have acquired a man through God." The preposition διά (through/by means of) makes the divine agency instrumental: God was the means by which the child came. This supports the "with the help of" reading in Greek.

---

### Verse 2 — "She gave birth again — his brother Abel"

The Hebrew is terse in a way that is narratively deliberate: וַתֹּסֶף לָלֶדֶת אֶת־אָחִיו אֶת־הָבֶל (vattosef laledet et-ahiv et-Havel). Literally: "And she added to give birth, his brother, Abel." There is no separate exclamation, no declaration, no announcement. Just: she gave birth again — Abel. The contrast with verse 1 is sharp. Cain's birth warranted a theological cry. Abel's birth gets three Hebrew words. This is not narrative carelessness. This is the first shadow.

**הֶבֶל (Hevel/Abel)** — the name means *breath*, *vapor*, *vanity*, *emptiness*, *a puff of air*. It is the same word Qohelet will use sixty-seven times in Ecclesiastes: "Vanity of vanities, all is vanity." Abel is named Breath. He is vapor. He will be here and then gone. The name is his fate. The Hebrew reader knows this from the moment the second son is introduced, and the dread accumulates in the space between his naming and his death.

---

### Verse 3 — "In the course of time" for מִקֵּץ יָמִים (miqqets yamim)

Literally "at the end of days" — an idiom for a period of time having passed. I render "in the course of time" to carry the natural rhythm of seasonal life without over-specifying. The offerings likely have an agricultural setting: harvest-time, the end of the growing season. The text does not say this, but מִקֵּץ יָמִים implies a natural interval has elapsed, a cycle has completed.

---

### Verses 4–5 — Why did God reject Cain's offering? The text's deliberate silence

The text does not say. This is not a textual problem. It is a narrative choice of the highest order. The traditions of interpretation have filled this silence for millennia: Cain brought inferior produce; Cain brought without faith; Cain's heart was already wrong; the order of offering matters (Abel first-fruits, Cain generic produce). The Masoretic text offers one subtle signal: Abel brought "from the firstborn of his flock, from their fat portions" — the best, the first, the prime cut. Cain brought "from the fruit of the ground" — no superlative, no qualification, no firstborn. A comparison is embedded in the description without being stated.

But the rejection precedes the fury, and the fury precedes the murder. Whether the rejection was just or capricious or opaque, it happened — and what happened next is what the chapter is about.

The LXX adds a revealing interpretive touch: in verse 7, God addresses Cain with **οὐκ ἐὰν ὀρθῶς προσενέγκῃς, ὀρθῶς δὲ μὴ διέλῃς, ἥμαρτες** — "Did you not sin if you offered rightly but did not divide rightly?" The LXX appears to add a ritual element: the manner of presenting the sacrifice mattered. The MT does not have this elaboration. My translation follows the MT's silence.

---

### Verse 5 — "Cain burned with fury, and his face fell"

The Hebrew וַיִּחַר לְקַיִן מְאֹד (vayyihar leQayin meod) is visceral: "it burned greatly for Cain." The idiom means rage — literally the heat of anger — and the adverb מְאֹד (meod, very/greatly) intensifies it. I render "burned with fury" to carry the physical heat of the Hebrew. He is not merely disappointed. Something ignites.

**וַיִּפְּלוּ פָּנָיו (vayyippelu fanav)** — "his face fell." This is the idiom for dejection, shame, humiliation. The face-fall answers the question in verse 6: "why has your face fallen?" Something in Cain's bearing, his countenance, collapsed when God did not look at his offering. The physical detail is precise: not abstract despair, but a visible change in his face. "His face fell" is the correct English idiom; I use it because it is idiomatic in both languages and the resonance is exact.

---

### Verse 7 — Sin crouching at the door: the broken warning

This is the most linguistically difficult verse in the chapter, and perhaps one of the most difficult verses in all of Genesis. The Hebrew of the warning's middle section is syntactically broken: לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל־בּוֹ (lappetach hattat rovets ve'eleykha teshuqato ve'attah timshol-bo).

Several problems at once:

**חַטָּאת (hattat)** is feminine in Hebrew. But **רֹבֵץ (rovets)** — "crouching" — is masculine. The grammatical mismatch is not a scribal error: it is a signal. "Sin" is personified as something not quite fixed in gender, not quite domesticated into a stable category. It is more animal than concept. The Akkadian cognate *rabisu* is a demon that crouches at doorways waiting to pounce. The Hebrew may carry this demonic undertone: sin is not an abstract moral failure. It is a predator at the threshold.

**תְּשׁוּקָה (teshuqah)** — the same word used in 3:16 for the woman's desire for her husband, and in Song of Solomon 7:10 for the lover's desire. Now it is sin that *desires* Cain, that *craves* him. Sin wants him the way a lover wants the beloved. This is not metaphor — or rather, it is metaphor deployed with total seriousness. Sin has appetite. It longs for Cain specifically. And Cain must master it.

**וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל־בּוֹ (ve'attah timshol-bo)** — "but you must master it." The same verb מָשַׁל (mashal) used in 3:16 where the man will "rule over" the woman. The same grammar of domination now applied to the relationship between Cain and sin: you must overpower it, you must be the one who rules. The warning is not gentle. It is urgent — almost frantic. God is telling Cain that what is crouching at the door is after him, and only he can stop it.

My rendering — "sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you — but you must master it" — tries to carry the predatory urgency without explaining it. The em dashes are pacing devices: they stagger the warning in the way the Hebrew staggering syntax does. The verse must feel *dangerous*. The reader should feel what Cain apparently did not feel: that something terrible is about to happen and there is still time to stop it.

The DSS fragment 4QGen-d preserves this verse with some orthographic variation but no significant textual divergence from the MT. The broken syntax is original.

---

### Verse 8 — "Cain said to his brother Abel, 'Let us go out to the field'"

The MT includes this speech — "Let us go out to the field" (נֵלְכָה הַשָּׂדֶה, nelkhah hassadeh) — but it is textually complex. The Samaritan Pentateuch, the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, and the Latin Vulgate all include this speech. The MT preserves it with some manuscripts leaving the quotation content slightly ambiguous. The LXX Vaticanus reads: **εἴπωμεν εἰς τὸ πεδίον** — "let us go out into the plain/field" — confirming the invitation.

What makes this detail devastating is what follows it. The invitation is a trap. Cain lures his brother out of sight — away from parents, away from any witness — and kills him there. The field is the first crime scene in human history.

The Hebrew for the murder is four words: וַיָּקָם קַיִן אֶל־הֶבֶל אָחִיו וַיַּהַרְגֵהוּ (vayyaqom Qayin el-Hevel ahiv vayyahargahu). "And Cain rose up against his brother Abel and killed him." No detail of how. No description of Abel's reaction. The narrator refuses to dwell on the violence and refuses to soften it. It happens in one sentence.

---

### Verse 9 — "Am I my brother's keeper?"

The Hebrew: הֲשֹׁמֵר אָחִי אָנֹכִי (hashomer ahi anokhi). Literally: "a keeper of my brother — am I?" The word שֹׁמֵר (shomer) means guardian, watchman, keeper — the one who watches over, who is responsible for, who looks after. It is the word used of the cherubim guarding the garden, of the LORD keeping his covenant, of the watchman on the city wall.

Cain's answer is a question, which means it is a denial dressed as a question, which means it is a lie dressed as rhetoric. He does not say "I don't know where he is." He says "I don't know" — then, before God can press further, pivots to a question that challenges the premise of divine inquiry itself: "Am I responsible for him? Is that my role?" The audacity is staggering. The man who just killed his brother stands before God and demands to know why tracking his brother's whereabouts should fall to him.

This is the line. Of all the lines in this chapter — Eve's declaration, Lamech's boast — this is the one that has lived longest in the human conscience. Not because it is wicked (though it is), but because it asks the question that every generation must answer.

"Keeper" is the right translation. Every other attempt to find a synonym — caretaker, guardian, minder — loses something. The word must be "keeper" because that is what it became.

---

### Verse 10 — "Your brother's blood — it is crying out to me from the ground!"

The Hebrew here is plural and shocking: דְּמֵי אָחִיךָ (demei ahikhah) — "the bloods of your brother." Not "blood" but "bloods." The plural of דָּם (dam, blood) in Hebrew is often used for shed blood, for blood that has been spilled violently, for the blood of victims. The plural sounds like quantity — as if Abel's blood is everywhere, soaking into the ground in multiple streams, crying out from multiple mouths simultaneously.

**צֹעֲקִים (tso'aqim)** — "crying out." This is the cry of the oppressed, the cry for justice, the same root as the cry that came up to God from Sodom (Genesis 18:20) and the cry of the Israelites in Egypt (Exodus 3:7). Blood has a voice. It speaks. The earth received it and is speaking it back to God. The ground is a witness.

I render "crying out" without softening: the present participle catches the ongoing, still-active cry of spilled blood. Abel is dead, but his blood has not stopped speaking.

---

### Verse 11 — "cursed are you from the ground" — the difference from Adam

Adam was told that the *ground* is cursed because of him. Cain is told that *he himself* is cursed from the ground. The direction of the curse has inverted. The ground was Adam's adversary; for Cain, the ground becomes his active rejecter. The earth that received his brother's blood will no longer receive Cain's labor. He is cursed *from* it — separated, expelled, cut off from the very thing that defined him. Cain was "a worker of the ground" (verse 2). Now the ground rejects him. His entire identity has been destroyed.

---

### Verse 12 — "A wanderer and a fugitive you will be on the earth"

The Hebrew: נָע וָנָד תִּהְיֶה בָאָרֶץ (na' vanad tihyeh va'arets). The two words נָע (na', wandering) and נָד (nad, fugitive/fugitive-exile) are near-rhymes in Hebrew, paired for poetic and sonic effect. I render "wanderer and fugitive" to carry both words. Some translations choose "restless wanderer" (NIV) or "fugitive and wanderer" (ESV). The order in Hebrew is wanderer first, fugitive second — but the pairing is a hendiadys of instability: a person cut loose from place, from home, from belonging, moving without rest.

The name נֹד (Nod, verse 16) derives from this same root נָד. The land of Nod is "the land of wandering" — which means Cain's punishment is written into the geography. He settles in the land of his own sentence.

---

### Verse 13 — "My punishment is too great to carry" — guilt or sentence?

The Hebrew: גָּדוֹל עֲוֹנִי מִנְּשֹׂא (gadol avoni minneso). This is the crux. The word עָוֹן (avon) carries two distinct but related meanings in Hebrew: **guilt** (the inward state of wrongdoing) and **punishment** (the outward consequence imposed). Both meanings are simultaneously present in the word, and the Masoretes did nothing to disambiguate. The ancient versions divided:

- The **LXX Vaticanus** reads: **μείζων ἡ αἰτία μου τοῦ ἀφεθῆναί με** — "my crime/guilt is too great to be forgiven." The LXX chooses *guilt*. This is Cain experiencing the weight of what he has done.
- The **Vulgate** reads: *maior est iniquitas mea quam ut veniam merear* — "my iniquity is greater than I deserve to be forgiven." Again: moral weight.
- But many English translations read *punishment*: "My punishment is more than I can bear" (NIV, ESV). Here Cain is not confessing; he is complaining. The sentence is too harsh.

Both readings produce a coherent Cain, but they produce a different Cain. If it is guilt — this is a rare flash of moral awareness, a recognition of what he has done. If it is punishment — Cain is still in deflection mode, still the man who answered "am I my brother's keeper?" with a smirk, now protesting the severity of his sentence rather than owning the act that earned it.

The Hebrew does not choose. I render "My punishment is too great to carry" because it is the reading that better serves the dramatic momentum: Cain moves immediately in verse 14 to argue the severity of the sentence — "you are driving me off the face of the ground... whoever finds me will kill me." This is not the language of a man confessing guilt; it is the language of a man negotiating his fate. The complaint reading fits the characterization. The guilt reading, while theologically attractive, sits in tension with what follows.

But I note: the Hebrew will not let either reading win completely. עָוֹן always carries both.

---

### Verse 14 — "from your face I will be hidden"

מִפָּנֶיךָ אֶסָּתֵר (mippaneykha essater). Literally: "from your face I will hide myself" — or "from your face I will be hidden." The same Hebrew root used in 3:8 when the man and woman "hid themselves" from the LORD's presence. But there, they hid voluntarily. Here, Cain is being hidden from God's face involuntarily — separation from the divine presence is part of the punishment. Or is Cain saying this is what he fears? The verse is Cain speaking, not God decreeing, so the hiding is Cain's interpretation of what his exile will mean.

Either way, the phrase is devastating in its echo: the first act of concealment from God was the man in the garden, afraid, hiding among the trees. Now his son uses the same word — and the distance from God has become structural, permanent, geographical. East of Eden. Land of Nod. Away from the face.

---

### Verse 15 — "the LORD put a mark on Cain"

The Hebrew אוֹת (ot) means sign, mark, token — the same word used for the rainbow in chapter 9, for the stars as signs in chapter 1. It is a covenant word: a sign of a promise. The mark of Cain is not a mark of shame. It is a mark of protection. It is God's guarantee written on Cain's body: do not touch this man. The sevenfold vengeance is the LORD's own pledged response to anyone who kills Cain.

There is something almost unbearable in this. God has cursed Cain. God has expelled him. And then God marks him with protection. The same God who received Abel's blood from the ground now ensures that Cain will not be killed in turn. Justice and mercy running simultaneously, in opposite directions.

The text does not describe the mark. We do not know what it looked like. The centuries of art and speculation that have given it visible form are all working from nothing — the text refuses to say.

---

### Verse 17 — "He was building a city"

Cain, the wanderer, the man condemned to restless exile, builds a city. The irony is architectural: the one who cannot be at home builds the first home — for others, for his son's name. The Hebrew uses a participle (בֹּנֶה, boneh) that implies ongoing action: he *was building*, he *kept building*. The city is being constructed over time, a prolonged act of defiance against or accommodation of his punishment.

The naming of the city after Enoch his son may be the first recorded act of naming something after a person — dedicating your building project to your heir, to your legacy. Cain may not have a place in the world, but he will make a place. He will carve it out. He will call it by his son's name.

---

### Verses 18–22 — The genealogy of Cain: civilization and its discontents

The genealogy moves fast — five generations in two verses — then slows to introduce three of Lamech's children as culture-heroes. Jabal: the father of pastoral nomadic life. Jubal: the father of music. Tubal-cain: the father of metalworking. These are the founders of civilization. And they are all Cain's descendants.

This is one of the most disturbing structural choices in Genesis 1–11. Every form of civilized human existence — farming, herding, city-building, music, metallurgy — is attributed to the line of the first murderer. The gifts of culture are gifts with blood on them. The text does not moralize this. It simply states it and moves on.

Tubal-cain's name may carry an echo of *Tubal* (a place name associated with metal-working peoples in the ancient Near East) and of Cain himself: he is Cain's final, darkest avatar. The forger of swords. And then, in verse 23, his father sings about using them.

---

### Verses 23–24 — Lamech's Song: the first poem of vengeance

This is poetry. The Masoretic text presents these verses in a form that differs from the surrounding prose, and the cantillation marks reflect the elevated, measured register of verse. I set it as verse because that is what it is — the first poem in the Bible that a human being composes and delivers.

The poem is called the "Song of the Sword" by some scholars, the "Taunt of Lamech" by others. It is neither a hymn nor a lament. It is a boast. Lamech is telling his wives — and through them, anyone listening — that he has killed a man for wounding him, a young man merely for striking him. Then he invokes the promise God made for Cain (sevenfold protection) and multiplies it by eleven: seventy-seven times over. If God promised to avenge Cain, Lamech will avenge himself. He does not need God's protection. He will supply his own.

The poem is the logical conclusion of the chapter's downward spiral. Cain killed in rage and received God's mercy-mark. Lamech kills in retaliation and declares himself beyond the need for mercy. The escalation is mathematical: 1 → 7 → 77.

**אָדָם (adam)** — the man Lamech killed. The Hebrew uses the generic word for "a human being." It is not a name. It is anyone. Any man. Any young man who crossed him. The casualness of it is part of the horror.

**יֶלֶד (yeled)** — "a young man," literally "a child/boy." The killed youth is unnamed, unidentified, collateral to Lamech's pride.

The address to the wives — "Adah and Zillah — hear my voice! / Wives of Lamech — listen!" — is a literary trope: the summons before a declaration, the demand for an audience. Lamech wants witnesses for his defiance. He performs his violence as much as he commits it.

The LXX renders the poem faithfully, preserving the parallel structure and the 7/77 escalation. The Greek **ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά** (seventy times seven) is this same phrase quoted by Jesus in Matthew 18:22 when Peter asks how many times to forgive — "up to seven times?" and Jesus answers "not seven, but seventy-seven times" (or seventy times seven, depending on manuscript tradition). Jesus takes Lamech's number of vengeance and converts it into the number of forgiveness. The allusion, if intended, reverses the poem completely.

---

### Verse 25 — "God has granted me another offspring" for שָׁת (Shet/Seth)

Eve names her third son Seth — שֵׁת (Shet) — with a wordplay on שִׁית (shit), to set, to place, to grant/appoint. "God has *granted* [שָׁת] me another offspring." The name is the verb: God-has-placed, God-has-appointed. Seth is the appointed replacement.

The phrase "in place of Abel, because Cain killed him" is Eve speaking the murder plainly for the first time. She names it. She does not soften it. The sentence is as direct in Hebrew as it sounds in English: "because Cain killed him." After chapters of narrative reticence about the actual act, the mother names it.

The word "again" — also in the verse — matters. Adam was intimate with his wife *again*. Life resumed. Grief did not have the last word. The word *again* is one of the most quietly hopeful words in the chapter.

---

### Verse 26 — "At that time, people began to call on the name of the LORD"

אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה (az hukhal liqro beshem YHWH). "Then it was begun to call in the name of the LORD." The verb is passive Hiphil of חָלַל (halal): "it was begun." Who began it? The text does not say. In the time of Enosh, in the generation of Seth's son, something started. People called on the name of the LORD.

This is the chapter's final note. The genealogy of Cain ends with Lamech's sword-song. The genealogy of Seth ends with prayer. Two lines, two destinations. The contrast is total and unannounced.

The Targum Onkelos famously reads this verse differently — as a sign of *profanation* rather than calling upon: "Then the sons of men began to call their idols by the name of the LORD." The same Hebrew consonants, read with a different vocalization, yield a radically different meaning. The MT reads this as the beginning of worship; the Aramaic tradition read it as the beginning of idolatry. Both readings are in the text's acoustic range. I follow the MT's straightforward reading: this is the start of something right, not wrong. The chapter needed to end with a breath of hope.

---

### Summary table — key translation choices

| Verse | Hebrew | PNEUMA rendering | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4:1 | קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת־יְהוָה | "I have acquired a man — with the LORD" | ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God") | Theologically explosive; em dash marks the declaration. |
| 4:2 | הֶבֶל (Hevel) | "Abel" | Αβελ | Name means vapor/breath/vanity — his fate in his name. |
| 4:7 | חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ | "sin is crouching" | ἁμαρτία | Grammatical gender mismatch preserved; Akkadian rabisu undertone. |
| 4:7 | תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ | "its desire is for you" | — | Same word as 3:16 — sin desires Cain as a lover desires. |
| 4:9 | הֲשֹׁמֵר אָחִי אָנֹכִי | "Am I my brother's keeper?" | μὴ φύλαξ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ μού εἰμι ἐγώ | "Keeper" — the only word. |
| 4:10 | דְּמֵי אָחִיךָ | "your brother's blood" (plural) | φωνὴ αἵματος | Plural "bloods" — multiple streams of shed blood crying out. |
| 4:13 | עֲוֹנִי | "My punishment" | αἰτία ("crime/guilt") | MT holds both readings; context favors punishment. |
| 4:23–24 | Lamech's Song | Verse format | Preserved poetically in LXX | First human-composed poem; escalating vengeance 7→77. |
| 4:25 | שֵׁת (Shet) | "Seth" | Σηθ | Name = "God has granted/appointed"; Eve names the murder plainly. |
| 4:26 | לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה | "to call on the name of the LORD" | ἐπικαλεῖσθαι τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου | Targum reads as idolatry-beginning; MT reads as worship-beginning. I follow MT. |

---

### On the dramatic shape of the whole — a translator's final note

Genesis 4 is the Bible's first story of human failure *east of Eden*, after the fall's consequences have been paid. The garden is closed. The question now is: what will humanity do with its freedom?

The answer, in this chapter, is kill, lie, bargain, survive, build, boast, and — at the very end, quietly, without fanfare — pray.

The chapter does not resolve the tension between the line of Cain (civilization, murder, escalating vengeance) and the line of Seth (replacement, hope, prayer). It leaves them both running. The reader knows which line leads to Abraham. But the reader also knows that the line of Cain produced the plow and the lyre and the forge. We are all, still, living in Cain's city, playing Jubal's instruments, tilling the fields that Cain's family worked out.

The blood still cries. The question is still waiting for an answer. Am I my brother's keeper?

— PNEUMA
